feedback.text: Dear European Commission, I welcome the initiative of the Digital Fairness Act and its ambition to address imbalances in the digital marketplace. However, I would like to draw attention to a critical and currently unregulated area: digital ownership of media, including video games, films, and other digital content. At present, consumers are often led to believe they are purchasing a product, when in reality they are only granted a revocable license. This creates a situation where companies retain full control over the availability and functionality of digital goods, while consumers bear all the risk. Providers can unilaterally withdraw access to purchased contentoften citing declining user numbers or reduced profitabilitywithout compensation or recourse for the user. This asymmetry is not only unfair but also environmentally unsustainable. As a personal example, I own a physical copy of the game Defiance for Xbox 360. In 2021, the servers were shut down, rendering the game completely unusable. The disc and packaging are now effectively waste. This illustrates how digital deactivation can turn even physical media into environmental burdens. I urge the Commission to include clear definitions and protections for digital ownership in the Digital Fairness Act. Consumers should have: The right to retain access to purchased digital content, even if online services are discontinued. Transparency about the nature of the purchase (license vs. ownership). Provisions for compensation or alternatives if access is revoked. Without such measures, the digital marketplace remains a one-sided playing field where multinational corporations enjoy all the benefits and consumers shoulder all the risks. This is not only a consumer rights issueit is a matter of digital sustainability and fairness. Sincerely, Oliver Teuber
